data.title: Fathom projects Elevate and START to drive margin expansion to over 10% of transactions by year-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fathom Holdings Inc. (FTHM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Marco Fregenal reported that despite challenging housing market conditions, the company executed on its long-term strategy, stating, “For the full year 2025, we generated $420 million in revenue, representing a 25% year-over-year growth, and our total transactions increased nearly 15%, driven in part by the addition of My Home Group and the continued addition of strong agents to our network.”
  • Fregenal emphasized strategic progress in expanding ancillary businesses, launching new programs, and partnerships, and strengthening the leadership team, including the addition of Lori Muller as President of Fathom Realty, replacing Samantha Giuggio.
  • Fregenal detailed new commission structures, with the launch of Edge, moving from an annual to a monthly agent fee and introducing a 7% split, alongside a new $250 transaction fee, aiming to “add a significant incremental gross profit before any benefit from a market recovery.”
  • The CEO highlighted that “both Elevate and START carry significantly higher gross profit margins, typically ranging from 20% to 50%” and projected these programs could represent at least 10% of total transaction volume by year-end and over 15% by the end of 2027.
  • Vice President of Finance Daniel Weinmann reported, “Fourth quarter revenue totaled $90.6 million, a 1.2% decrease year-over-year compared to $91.7 million in the prior year period… For the full year 2025, total revenue increased 25.4% to $420.5 million compared to $335.2 million in 2024.”

Outlook

  • Fregenal stated that the company’s pricing and fee changes are already in effect and “fundamentally improve our unit economics at any level of transaction volume.”
  • “Looking ahead, our goal is for these two programs to represent at least 10% of our total transaction volume by year-end and increase to over 15% by the end of 2027,” Fregenal explained regarding Elevate and START.
  • The CEO indicated plans to expand the START program into 10 states by the end of 2026 and scale lead generation to more than 20,000 leads per month by year-end.
  • Fregenal reaffirmed that Fathom “expect[s] to deliver better margins and greater operating leverage” even without a market recovery, with additional upside if market conditions improve.

Financial Results

  • Weinmann reported, “Gross profit for the fourth quarter of 2025 increased to $7.1 million compared to $6.7 million in the fourth quarter of 2024… Gross profit margin for the fourth quarter of 2025 increased to 8.1% compared to 7.2% in the fourth quarter of 2024.”
  • GAAP net loss for the fourth quarter was $6.7 million or $0.21 per share, compared with $6.2 million or $0.29 per share for the fourth quarter of 2024. The year-over-year increase in net loss was primarily driven by a lower income tax benefit and a $900,000 loss on the sale of business.
  • Adjusted EBITDA loss for Q4 improved to $2.6 million compared to $2.9 million in Q4 2024. For the full year, adjusted EBITDA loss was $4 million compared to $5.7 million in 2024.
  • The brokerage segment closed 8,501 real estate transactions in Q4, a decrease from 9,903 in Q4 2024. Mortgage revenue rose to $3.4 million, up from $2 million in the prior year period, while title revenue increased to $1.8 million from $1.3 million.
  • The company ended Q4 with $5.7 million in cash and entered a $2 million financing arrangement to provide additional liquidity.

Q&A

  • Thomas Hayes, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC, Research Division: Asked about Elevate program targets for 2026. Fregenal responded, “Our goal by the end of the year is to have about 1,000 agents on Elevate. And I think combined right now, we're about 260, 275.”
  • Hayes inquired about agent feedback on the Edge program and margin contribution. Fregenal answered, “The new program, Fathom Edge starts on April 1 as well as the $250 brokerage fee… all new agents starting on April 1 will go into Fathom Edge.”
  • Hayes requested an update on the ByOwner partnership. Fregenal responded, “We already are connected with them. We're already getting leads from them… The ByOwner platform is going to be a meaningful platform for us as we get into Q2 and beyond this year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on adoption rates and margin improvements, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking clarity on program rollouts and partnerships without expressing skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, repeatedly emphasizing expected improvements in margins and profitability, using phrases such as “we believe,” “we expect,” and “we are excited.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence appears slightly higher, reflecting the implementation of new structural changes and an optimistic outlook on upcoming growth initiatives. Analysts’ tone shifted from curiosity about technology expansion in Q3 to interest in margin expansion and agent programs in Q4.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized margin expansion and agent-focused programs, with detailed implementation of the Edge commission plan and new fee structures, compared to Q3’s focus on revenue growth and technology licensing.
  • Guidance language shifted from general momentum and diversification to concrete targets for Elevate and START’s contribution to transaction volume.
  • Analysts shifted focus from technology platform commercialization and attach rates in Q3 to margin-driving initiatives and agent feedback in Q4.
  • Management’s confidence increased, highlighting structural improvements and profitability potential regardless of market recovery, compared to Q3’s cautious optimism tied to broader market trends.
  • Key metrics such as gross profit margin and adjusted EBITDA loss showed improvement, while transaction volumes declined, reflecting continued market headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Fregenal acknowledged persistent housing market headwinds, including higher interest rates and affordability constraints reducing transaction activity.
  • Weinmann cited “continued softness in the real estate market,” impacting transaction volumes and agent retention.
  • The company noted potential attrition from agents who do not generate transactions as a result of the new monthly fee, but stated, “removing these agents will have zero negative impact on our net income or EBITDA.”
  • Increased investment in technology and infrastructure, as well as accrued legal expenses and a loss on the sale of a business, impacted bottom-line results.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that Fathom’s strategic changes—including new fee structures, commission plans, and expansion of high-margin programs like Elevate and START—are designed to drive margin expansion and profitability, regardless of broader housing market conditions. The company aims to have Elevate and START contribute at least 10% of transaction volume by year-end, while continuing to invest in agent experience, customer satisfaction, and technology. Structural changes are expected to improve unit economics and position Fathom for meaningful upside as market conditions improve or stabilize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lantern Pharma outlines $15B–$20B market potential as AI-driven pipeline advances and funding needs intensify
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lantern Pharma (LTRN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Panna Sharma, President, CEO & Director, highlighted, "2025 was a defining year for Lantern Pharma. We achieved clinical validation, we believe, across multiple programs while establishing the foundation for our next phase of growth." Sharma stated that the company reached "encouraging and a unique development with LP-300 in the Phase II HARMONIC observations, combined with also a successful Phase Ia completion for our LP-184 clinical trial and most recently, an FDA IND clearance for our pediatric CNS cancer program through Starlight Therapeutics."
  • Sharma addressed rumors regarding his departure: "This is categorically untrue and appears to be rooted in a deliberate and perhaps malicious attempt to manipulate our stock price."
  • Sharma discussed the advancement of the HARMONIC trial for LP-300, noting the completion of targeted enrollment in Japan ahead of schedule and ongoing patient enrollment in the U.S., Japan, and Taiwan. He shared that the Phase II trial has shown an "86% clinical benefit rate and a 43% objective response rate in its initial safety lead-in cohort, including 1 patient with a durable complete response and survival continuing for nearly 2 years."
  • Sharma announced a Type C meeting with the FDA scheduled for mid-May 2026, seeking feedback on protocol amendments for LP-300, focusing on patient enrollment with the EGFR exon 21 L858R mutation, increasing treatment cycles, and moving to a single-arm Simon 2-stage design.
  • On LP-184, Sharma reported "a 48% clinical benefit rate at or above the therapeutic threshold" in a Phase I trial with 63 patients and emphasized mechanistic validation for DNA damage repair mutations.
  • Starlight Therapeutics received FDA IND clearance for a Phase I pediatric CNS cancer trial, with STAR-001 gaining multiple rare pediatric disease and orphan drug designations, providing Lantern the potential to obtain FDA priority review vouchers.
  • On the AI front, Sharma introduced the commercial launch of withZeta, a "multi-agentic system aimed at conquering rare cancers," and discussed the establishment of an AI center of excellence in India.
  • CFO David Margrave stated, "Our general and administrative expenses were approximately $1.5 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to approximately $1.6 million in the prior year period. R&D expenses were approximately $2.7 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to approximately $4.3 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. We recorded a net loss of approximately $4.1 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 or $0.36 per share compared to a net loss of approximately $5.9 million or $0.54 per share for the fourth quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • The company anticipates advancing clinical programs, expanding the RADR platform's commercial reach, and focusing on the withZeta platform launch at the AACR in the next month.
  • Management indicated a need to raise "substantial additional funding in the near future," and current cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities are expected to fund operations until "at least approximately late July 2026 to mid-September 2026."
  • Sharma said, "We'll also be pursuing additional funding, including potential grant revenue to fund planned operations and clinical advancement of our precision oncology trials."

Financial Results

  • R&D expenses for the quarter were $2.7 million, general and administrative expenses were $1.5 million, and net loss for the quarter was ($4.1 million) or ($0.36) per share.
  • For the full year, R&D expenses were $11.5 million, general and administrative expenses $6.5 million, and net loss was ($17.1 million) or ($1.57) per share.
  • Cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities stood at $10.1 million as of December 31, 2025.
  • CFO Margrave said, "Our R&D expenses continue to exceed our G&A expenses by a strong margin, reflecting our focus on advancing our product candidates and pipeline."

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: The transcript did not contain a live analyst Q&A; participants were instructed to submit questions via email or an online tool, and no analyst exchange is included in the transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • There is no analyst input in the call transcript; therefore, direct sentiment from analysts cannot be assessed.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, using phrases such as "We believe that we had encouraging and a unique development" and "we're laser-focused on executing our dual engine strategy."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains positive and forward-looking, with continued emphasis on AI-driven innovation, clinical advancement, and commercial opportunities, but now also explicitly addressing public rumors about leadership stability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance now includes a more urgent tone regarding the need for "substantial additional funding in the near future," compared to the previous quarter’s expectation of adequate cash into Q3 2026.
  • Strategic focus has evolved, with greater emphasis on the commercial rollout of withZeta and expansion of the AI business model, compared to prior emphasis on clinical trial progress.
  • Key financial metrics show a decrease in both R&D expenses and net loss compared to the previous quarter, reflecting further cost discipline.
  • Management tone remains confident and future-focused, but the direct address of CEO departure rumors signals heightened sensitivity to investor sentiment.
  • No analyst Q&A this quarter, in contrast to the previous quarter where analysts posed questions about trial milestones, Zeta development, and clinical program timelines.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management explicitly warned of the need for substantial additional funding and a limited operational runway, with cash only expected to last until late July 2026 to mid-September 2026.
  • CEO Sharma addressed the impact of “malicious and fake news” regarding management stability, noting harm to the company and investors, and announced plans to pursue legal action.
  • The advancement of planned clinical trials and commercial initiatives is contingent on securing new funding.

Final Takeaway

Lantern Pharma’s management emphasized that the company’s AI-driven clinical pipeline and the commercial launch of withZeta position it for future growth, with a combined market potential exceeding $15 billion and approaching $20 billion. Management underscored a disciplined reduction in operating expenses and highlighted key clinical and regulatory milestones, but also acknowledged the need to secure substantial new funding to sustain operations and progress clinical and commercial initiatives into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. crude oil closes above $100 for first time since 2022 after latest Middle East threats
data.description:
U.S. crude oil futures settled Monday above $100/bbl for the first time since July 2022 after President Trump threatened to blow up Iran's power plants, oil wells, and the Kharg Island oil export hub "if for any reason a deal is not shortly reached" to end the Middle East war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz trade route.

At the same time, Trump said in a post on Truth Social that the U.S. was engaged in "serious discussions" with "a new and more reasonable regime" to end military operations in Iran.

Adding more uncertainty to a diplomatic end to the war, The Wall Street Journal reported the Trump administration is considering a plan to extract nearly 1,000 lbs of uranium from Iran.

Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen launched missiles at Israel over the weekend, raising fears that shipping in the Red Sea could be attacked at the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, another crucial chokepoint for global oil trading.

A successful attempt by the Houthis to disrupt flows through the Red Sea would be "catastrophic" for oil supplies out of the region, as "further production shut-ins by Saudi would then be very likely," senior oil market analyst June Goh of Sparta Commodities said in a note.

"While the rhetoric out of the U.S. and Israel suggests both countries are ahead of plans, the reality is that Iran's ability to create havoc in the oil markets has not been defeated. This remains a real concern for oil inflation infiltrating the global economy," Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital said in a note.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery closed up 3.2% to $102.88/bbl, its highest settlement value since July 19, 2022, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for May posted only a slight gain, up 0.2%, to $112.78/bbl, but it was enough for the benchmark's best settlement value since July 4, 2022.

After-hours, Nymex crude rose another 3% to ~$106/bbl following reports that Iran attacked a fully laden Kuwaiti crude carrier in Dubai Port, causing a fire and potential oil spill in surrounding waters.

U.S. natural gas futures fell as the market enters the low-demand spring shoulder season, when heating use drops and cooling demand has yet to get going; the front-month Nymex May contract finished down 4.6% to $2.887/MMBtu, its lowest settlement value since February 27.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Progress Software outlines $988M–$1B FY26 revenue target as AI drives operational gains and debt reduction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Progress Software Corporation (PRGS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Yogesh Gupta reported "another very good quarter" with revenue of $248 million, a 4% increase year-over-year, and emphasized that "EPS for the quarter was $1.60, up 22% year-over-year as operating margins finished above 41%." Gupta highlighted record cash flows, stating that "adjusted free cash flow was $99 million and unlevered free cash flow was $111 million," attributing these results to "strong focus on collections" and the impact of AI-driven innovation across the product portfolio.
  • Gupta explained that “AI has enabled us to accelerate our innovation cycles as well as helped us transform our product capabilities to be more relevant for the future,” citing customer examples in government, finance, and HR using Progress’s agentic RAG and data platform for measurable business outcomes.
  • The CEO also outlined key capital allocation actions: "In Q1, we paid down $60 million in debt and repurchased $20 million of stock." ShareFile's integration was cited as a significant contributor to recurring revenue and operational efficiency.
  • Gupta announced the opening of a new innovation hub in Bangalore, consolidating former Progress and ShareFile offices and reinforcing the company’s global growth and innovation strategy.
  • CFO Anthony Folger stated, "We closed Q1 with ARR of approximately $863 million, representing 2% pro forma year-over-year growth," and confirmed "net retention rate remains strong, coming in at 99%." Folger commented on disciplined cost management, with total costs and operating expenses at $146 million, which was "largely flat compared to the year ago quarter." He reported operating income of $102 million and an operating margin of 41%.

Outlook

  • Folger provided guidance for Q2 2026, expecting revenue between $240 million and $246 million and EPS of $1.47–$1.53. For the full year 2026, anticipated revenue is between $988 million and $1 billion, operating margin of approximately 39%, adjusted free cash flow between $263 million and $275 million, unlevered free cash flow between $315 million and $326 million, and EPS between $5.91 and $6.03. The company plans to continue focusing on debt repayment and share repurchases, aiming for a net leverage ratio of approximately 2.7x by year-end.
  • Management reiterated that "ARR growth to be generally in line with revenue growth for the full year."

Financial Results

  • Reported Q1 revenue was $248 million, a 4% year-over-year increase. EPS for the quarter was $1.60. Adjusted free cash flow reached $99 million, up from $73 million in the prior year quarter. Deferred revenue at quarter-end was $425 million, up $25 million year-over-year. DSO improved to 52 days from 73 days in Q4. The company ended the quarter with $113 million in cash and total debt of $1.35 billion, bringing net debt to approximately $1.24 billion. The net leverage ratio was 3.1x, with a plan for further reduction in 2026.

Q&A

  • Ittai Kidron, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., asked about the pace of M&A activity. Gupta responded, "There is a clearly a higher bar today given where our own company stock is and our valuation is compared to what it historically was," noting constraints on what the company can pay for acquisitions and a disconnect between private and public market valuations.
  • Kidron also inquired about the decline in SaaS revenue and isolated churn. Folger explained the churn as "customer-specific events that weren't really related to product value or competitive dynamics," citing a terminated government contract due to a court ruling. He attributed SaaS revenue variability to ShareFile billing system integration and assured that "the positive aspects that we saw, especially this quarter were enhanced collections."
  • John DiFucci, Guggenheim Securities, raised questions about developer seat count and ShareFile performance. Gupta clarified, "absolute numbers have dropped in what I would call a small number of customers," but noted overall developer products "are doing well" due to significant AI investments.
  • DiFucci pressed on SaaS revenue trends. Folger maintained that "$72 million to $73 million for total SaaS revenue on a quarterly basis is where it's been" and expected future fluctuations to diminish as system integration is completed.
  • Lucky Schreiner, D.A. Davidson, asked about visibility on multiyear contracts and the impact of isolated churn. Gupta described the event as "a surprise churn," due to a sudden regulatory change, and maintained that visibility "hasn't changed."
  • Schreiner also questioned what it would take to bring NRR above 100%. Gupta replied, "I don't feel that today macro is playing a factor for our business...this is purely because of the isolated churn that we saw." He reiterated, "Our target continues to be being at or above 100%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts questioned the pace of M&A and raised concerns about SaaS revenue variability and isolated churn, reflecting a slightly negative tone and persistent skepticism, especially regarding ShareFile and billing integration.
  • Management maintained a confident and methodical tone, emphasizing strong fundamentals and operational discipline. Gupta highlighted, "the business is performing well. The model remains durable. AI is making our products and operations stronger."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the current management tone was consistent in confidence, but analysts appeared more focused on SaaS revenue normalization and M&A timing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year revenue and EPS is similar to the prior quarter, maintaining a range of $988 million to $1 billion in revenue and EPS of $5.91–$6.03 (previous quarter: $986 million to $1 billion revenue and $5.82–$5.96 EPS).
  • Operating margin guidance remains at 39% for the year. ARR growth target is consistent at 2% year-over-year. Management continues to prioritize debt reduction and disciplined capital allocation.
  • Analysts in the prior quarter emphasized AI monetization and operating margin drivers, while the current quarter saw more questions regarding SaaS revenue normalization and isolated churn events.
  • Management's confidence in AI-driven innovation and capital discipline remained unchanged from the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Isolated churn events, such as the termination of a significant government contract due to regulatory changes, were highlighted. Folger described these as "not because of any dissatisfaction with our product or competitive loss, but the underlying use case effectively gets eliminated by a court rule."
  • Integration of ShareFile’s billing system was noted as a source of revenue variability, with management indicating that most cleanup is completed and future fluctuations should be limited.
  • Management continues to monitor macroeconomic and geopolitical risks, but stated, “we have not seen any instance or any example or any anecdotal evidence, anything at all to say that macro is having an impact on us.”

Final Takeaway

Progress Software delivered another quarter of revenue and earnings growth, driven by ongoing AI innovation and disciplined capital allocation. The company continues to prioritize operational efficiency, debt reduction, and shareholder returns, while addressing isolated churn events and SaaS revenue normalization. Management remains confident in the durability of the business model, with guidance reaffirmed for steady growth and margin performance through fiscal 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ferroglobe warns it may shut South Africa operations due to soaring electricity costs
data.description:
Ferroglobe (GSM) finished down 3.2% on Monday after warning it may be forced to shut down all its South Africa operations within days, citing a prolonged and unprecedented escalation in electricity prices.

The silicon metal and ferroalloy producer said it would have no alternative but to halt production across its facilities unless reduced electricity tariffs are approved before April 1, with dismissals of all remaining staff likely to take effect starting in June.

Ferroglobe (GSM) said its electricity prices have increased by more than 900% since 2007 and now account for more than half of its total production cost, pushing production costs above selling prices and creating a severe disadvantage compared to international producers operating in regions where electricity prices are lower.

The company said a new tariff increase of nearly 9% by Eskom, South Africa's state-owned, primary electricity public utility, would deepen already severe financial losses.

Ferroglobe (GSM) has built a significant industrial footprint over nearly three decades in South Africa, with assets including the Polokwane silicon metal smelter and the eMalahleni ferrosilicon smelter that have already suffered cutbacks in recent years.

data.title: CVD Equipment outlines $1.8M cost reduction plan and $16.9M asset sale amid transformation strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CVD Equipment Corporation (CVV) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Emmanuel Lakios, President and CEO, stated that the company has "initiated a transformation strategy during the fourth quarter designed to significantly reduce fixed operating costs, create a more agile organization and better position the company to maximize shareholder value." Key actions include transitioning from a vertically integrated fabrication model to outsourcing, workforce reduction in the CVD Equipment division expected to save approximately $1.8 million in 2026, revising sales by leveraging distributors, and considering strategic alternatives such as asset sales.
  • Lakios also announced, "on March 23, 2026, we announced that we had entered into a definitive agreement under which our SDC business will be sold to Atlas Copco Group. The purchase price is approximately $16.9 million in cash, subject to certain purchase price adjustments." Net cash proceeds after expenses and taxes are expected to be about $15 million, with $900,000 in escrow.
  • Richard Catalano, Executive VP and CFO, commented, "Fourth quarter 2025 revenues were $5 million. This compares to $7.4 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. This year-over-year decline was primarily driven by lower CVD systems revenue." He also highlighted that the SDC segment reported $2.2 million in the quarter.

Outlook

  • The company expects the cost reductions and asset sale to strengthen the balance sheet and provide "additional financial flexibility as we continue to evaluate opportunities across the CVD Equipment business, its product lines and our facilities," according to Lakios.
  • Catalano noted, "our return to consistent profitability will depend on improved equipment order flow, disciplined cost management, successful execution of our transformation plan and continued control of capital expenditures."
  • The company plans to invest net cash proceeds from the SDC sale in U.S. treasury securities initially.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter 2025 revenue was reported at $5 million, down 33% sequentially and from the prior year period.
  • Full year 2025 revenue was $25.8 million, a decrease of 4.1% from fiscal year 2024.
  • Orders in Q4 totaled $3.5 million, with full year orders at $13 million compared to $28 million in 2024.
  • Backlog as of December 31, 2025, was $6.6 million versus $8 million at the end of September and $19.4 million at the end of December 2024.
  • Consolidated gross profit for Q4 was $1.1 million (22.2% margin), down from $2 million (26.4%) a year prior.
  • Q4 operating loss was $1.3 million compared to operating income of $34,000 in Q4 2024. A noncash impairment charge of $163,000 was recorded in Q4 2025.
  • Net loss for Q4 was $1.3 million or ($0.18) per diluted share, compared with net income of $132,000 or $0.02 per diluted share in the prior year quarter.
  • Cash and cash equivalents at year end stood at $8.7 million, down from $12.6 million a year prior. Working capital improved to $14.1 million from $13.8 million.

Q&A

  • Brett Reiss, Janney Montgomery Scott LLC, asked about the skill sets of engineers and potential acquisitions with the cash on hand. Emmanuel Lakios responded that the “talent pool is consistent with what the talent pool was essentially from a capabilities perspective a year ago,” and the Board is “looking at opportunities and strategic alternatives for increasing shareholder value,” but nothing material is in progress yet.
  • Reiss followed up on the pipeline of opportunities. Lakios said the Board has “been looking at strategic alternatives for quite several quarters,” but currently, “I don't have anything to speak of.”
  • Reiss inquired about prospects for more recurring or service revenue. Lakios acknowledged, “the objective for any strategic activity, which we want to have is have a smooth non-lumpy revenue stream... Those are all the attributes of entities we would like to entertain. But again, I can't speak to that at this point.”
  • Frank Giordano, Private Investor, asked if a special dividend was considered. Lakios responded, “I do not believe that in the history of the company, a special dividend was paid... at this point in time, that is not actively on the table.”
  • Giordano also asked about focus on military and defense. Lakios stated, “about 78% of our revenue over the last several years of our orders has come from military and defense.” He also cited recent orders for research systems used in hypersonics and next-generation materials.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on capital allocation, the use of proceeds from the asset sale, and the potential for more predictable revenue streams, indicating a slightly negative to neutral sentiment regarding near-term growth prospects and strategic clarity.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly defensive tone, emphasizing ongoing strategic reviews and transformation initiatives but providing few specifics or commitments. The use of phrases like “I can't speak to that at this point” and “we'll continue to do that” reflect caution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism increased due to softer results and a lack of clear forward-looking commitments, while management’s tone shifted from optimistic on transformation to more measured and guarded.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a 33% drop in revenue from the previous quarter, a widening operating loss, and a drop in backlog from both the prior quarter and year-end 2024. The transformation strategy, including outsourcing and cost reduction, was implemented this quarter, whereas last quarter it was only announced.
  • The SDC asset sale is a new strategic move compared to Q3, and guidance language shifted from focusing on growth opportunities to prioritizing cost control and financial flexibility.
  • Analysts this quarter were more focused on capital deployment and recurring revenue, whereas last quarter’s questions centered on operational details and outsourcing rationale.
  • Management confidence was more measured, with a focus on navigating uncertain demand and maximizing shareholder value through strategic alternatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "continued volatility in order rates and recent decline in bookings," "softer demand for our products in our CVD Equipment division," tariff-related uncertainties, reduced U.S. government spending for universities, and a "slower pace of adoption of our solutions in certain end markets."
  • The company is "monitoring our customer demand, the general uncertainty of the geopolitical environment and potential tariff impacts" and adjusting its transformation strategy accordingly.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about lumpy revenue, capital allocation, and the lack of a clear pipeline for recurring revenue or acquisitions.

Final Takeaway

CVD Equipment Corporation highlighted the execution of a significant transformation strategy in Q4 2025, aiming to reduce fixed costs by $1.8 million and refocus the business through outsourcing and divestiture of the SDC segment for $16.9 million. Financial performance reflected ongoing challenges, with revenues, backlog, and order flow all declining, and the company operating at a loss. Management maintained its commitment to shareholder value and strategic flexibility, but near-term visibility remains limited as the company navigates industry headwinds and explores further opportunities for recurring revenue and growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oxbridge targets 20% and 42% token returns while expanding into AI data center revenue streams
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oxbridge Re Holdings Limited (OXBR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Sanjay Madhu, Chairman, President & CEO, highlighted significant progress in bringing reinsurance on chain and broadening investor access. He stated, "We are proud of the significant steps we have taken to fortify and innovate our business by bringing reinsurance on chain and broadening investor access." Madhu emphasized Oxbridge's disciplined underwriting focus on low frequency, high severity risk, supported by full collateralization and rigorous data analysis. He described SurancePlus as a key platform expanding compliant and scalable access to reinsurance as a real-world asset, mentioning, "SurancePlus continues to expand our ability to bring reinsurance on chain in a compliant and scalable manner, broadening access to an asset class that has historically been limited to institutional partnerships."
  • Madhu further announced expansion into the Solana ecosystem and broader blockchain distribution enabled by Layer 0 through Alphaledger, stating these developments position SurancePlus within leading blockchain environments for real-world asset adoption.
  • Wrendon Timothy, CFO, reported, "Net premiums earned for the 3 months ended December 31, 2025, decreased to $555,000 from $595,000 for the quarter ended December 31, 2024...Net income for the quarter ended December 31, 2025, was $120,000 or $0.02 per basic and diluted income per share compared to a net loss of $460,000 or $0.05 basic and diluted loss per share for the quarter ended December 31, 2024."
  • Madhu also revealed, "The balance yield token is tracking 25% ahead of its 20% target, and the high-yield token is tracking its 42% target." He announced plans to target returns of 20% and 42% for the T20 and T42 offerings in the 2026-2027 contract cycle, and discussed exploring tokenization of data center revenue streams linked to AI growth.

Outlook

  • Oxbridge is targeting returns of 20% and 42% for its T20 and T42 tokenized reinsurance offerings for the upcoming cycle. Madhu stated, "As we look ahead to the 2026, 2027 contract cycle, we are targeting returns of 20% and 42% for our T20 and T42 offerings."
  • Management expressed optimism about a favorable risk environment due to El Nino conditions and signaled ongoing exploration of new tokenization opportunities in cash-generating assets, including data center revenue streams.

Financial Results

  • Net premiums earned for Q4 2025 were $555,000, down from $595,000 in Q4 2024, due to lower average rates on reinsurance contracts.
  • Total revenue for the quarter was $576,000, up from $422,000 in the prior year period.
  • Net income for Q4 2025 was $120,000 or $0.02 per share, compared to a net loss of $460,000 or $0.05 per share in Q4 2024. For the full year, net loss was $2.08 million or $0.28 per share versus $2.73 million or $0.45 per share in 2024.
  • Total expenses for Q4 2025 increased to $1.04 million from $497,000, primarily due to underwriting losses from Hurricane Milton and higher administrative costs.
  • The combined ratio for Q4 2025 was 187.6%, up from 83.5% in Q4 2024, reflecting increased loss and expense ratios due to adverse loss development and higher costs.
  • Cash and restricted cash increased to approximately $7 million at year end, up from $5.89 million, primarily due to new collateral deposits for the current treaty year.

Q&A

  • Kent Engelke, Capitol Securities Management, Inc.: "Can you expand a little bit more about when you're talking about the tokenization of artificial intelligence infrastructure...can you expand on the tokenization of data center revenue?" Sanjay Madhu explained that Oxbridge is evaluating strategic relationships with partners and operators to explore tokenization of data center revenues, noting potential significant value for both Oxbridge and SurancePlus.
  • Engelke: "It appears as though you have plenty of cash to go forward and the like. Am I reading that correctly in regards to your cash balances and your restricted cash?" Madhu responded, "Yes. We have about $6.9 million in cash and restricted cash. That puts us in great position not only to do things with the reinsurance tokenization, but also to evaluate other opportunities."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was positive and focused on forward-looking opportunities, such as the tokenization of AI-related revenue streams and the company's liquidity position.
  • Management maintained an optimistic and confident tone throughout, expressing encouragement about current performance and future expansion. Madhu used phrases like "we are encouraged by the performance" and "we are optimistic about the opportunities these presents."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analyst sentiment remained positive, but the current call placed greater emphasis on new strategic initiatives and future growth prospects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language evolved from focusing on expanding in the Web3 and RWA sector to specifically targeting 20% and 42% returns for upcoming tokenized offerings and pursuing new asset tokenization opportunities.
  • Strategic focus shifted from reinforcing core operations and platform enhancements to exploring data center revenue tokenization and expanding blockchain partnerships.
  • Analysts' questions this quarter centered on the company's new AI infrastructure initiatives and liquidity, while the previous quarter saw no analyst engagement.
  • Key metrics showed improvement in net income for Q4 2025 over Q4 2024, despite higher expenses and adverse loss development.
  • Management expressed continued optimism, with increased confidence in scaling platform capabilities and asset diversification.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company cited adverse loss development from Hurricane Milton as a significant driver of increased expenses and higher combined ratios.
  • Management highlighted higher professional, legal, and tokenization-related costs as contributors to elevated expense ratios.
  • No specific mitigation strategies were detailed beyond disciplined underwriting and broadening platform capabilities.

Final Takeaway

Oxbridge Re Holdings signaled ongoing momentum in its on-chain reinsurance platform and set ambitious targets of 20% and 42% returns for its next cycle of tokenized offerings. Management pointed to successful expansion into blockchain ecosystems, a strengthened cash position, and new initiatives to tokenize data center revenue streams associated with AI infrastructure. The company remains committed to disciplined underwriting and platform scalability while addressing challenges from catastrophe losses and rising operational costs, positioning itself for long-term shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: HireQuest signals market stabilization, launches $20M share repurchase and MRI restructuring
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: HireQuest, Inc. (HQI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Richard Hermanns opened the call by acknowledging persistent macro challenges in the staffing industry but emphasized, "we remain solidly profitable and executed well in 2025." Hermanns outlined a major strategic development: "we announced our strategic decision to change the ownership structure of MRI network by divesting the permanent placement piece of the business into a new entity in transitioning majority ownership to a newly formed leadership group made up of current and former franchise owners." He described this move as aligning MRI’s leadership with those "who live its mission every day," while HireQuest retains partial ownership and continues operating the contract staffing segment.
  • Hermanns announced a share repurchase program: "HireQuest Board of Directors had approved a share repurchase program that authorizes the company to repurchase up to $20 million of its outstanding shares of common stock." He stated, "We believe that a share repurchase program is currently an efficient use of our capital, reflects our commitment to prudent capital management and deployment and reinforces the confidence that the Board and management team have in HireQuest long-term strategy while also returning capital to our shareholders."
  • The CEO highlighted findings from a survey of over 400 offices, noting that "68% of offices surveyed set time to fill for open rolls steadied in 2025, while 35% saw increases," suggesting market stability. He also observed, "61% of recruiters expect the time to fill to remain stable in 2026, while 15% expect improvement as candidate supply normalizes."
  • CFO C. Hartley reported, "Total revenue in the fourth quarter of 2025 was $7 million compared with revenue of $8.1 million in the prior year, a decrease of 13%. For the full year, total revenue was $30.6 million compared to $34.6 million in 2024." Hartley further broke down the revenue drivers and expense controls, noting, "Franchise royalties for the quarter were $6.6 million compared to $7.6 million for the same quarter last year."
  • Hartley highlighted progress in reducing workers’ compensation expense: "net workers' compensation expense... totaled $89,000 for the full year compared with about $2 million in the full year of 2024, a decrease of $1.9 million." He emphasized strong liquidity, "we ended 2025 with about $33 million in working capital compared to $25.1 million at the end of the year in 2024," and a debt-free balance sheet.

Outlook

  • Hermanns expects "more balance in the labor market that appears to be stabilizing around new priorities including flexibility, fit and the kind of skilled work and labor that can't be automated by AI." He identified "AI and automation, reshoring and tariff relief and economic and political shifts" as key trends for 2026.
  • Hermanns stated, "we believe that we're well positioned with our franchise staffing model to benefit from a stabilizing market and to meet the shifting demands of employers in 2026."

Financial Results

  • Hartley reported, "Net income after tax was $1.6 million in the fourth quarter or $0.11 per diluted share compared to net income of $2.2 million or $0.16 per diluted share last year. For the full year, net income was $6.3 million or $0.45 per diluted share compared to $3.7 million or $0.26 per diluted share in 2024."
  • Adjusted net income for Q4 2025 was $2.7 million or $0.19 per diluted share, flat with Q4 2024. Full-year adjusted net income was $10 million or $0.71 per diluted share, also flat year-over-year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $3.4 million for the quarter and $14.1 million for the year, both down from prior year figures.
  • Hartley noted, "we have paid a regularly -- regular quarterly dividend since the third quarter of 2020. Most recently, we paid a $0.06 per common share dividend on March 16, 2025."

Q&A

  • Kevin Steinke, Barrington Research: Asked if market stabilization benefits any specific divisions more. Hermanns responded, "it hasn't necessarily been more pronounced in any particular division, but it's very apparent, and it's carried through into the first quarter. So the market has definitely, throughout the quarter, it is definitely seems to have found its bottom."
  • Steinke inquired about the MRI transaction’s impact on financials. Hermanns explained, "about 35% to 40% of, let's say, from 2025 of what we had in MRI has been retained via the contract staffing... the perm placement division was breakeven at best. So from an actual income standpoint, the effect will literally be nothing should be nothing." Hartley added, "in 2025, the executive search portion of MRI contributed about $65 million of system-wide sales and just a touch under $2 million for royalties."
  • Steinke sought updates on acquisitions. Hermanns replied, "in the middle of the fourth quarter, we had one that we were hopeful... then they got cold feet... we've had a bit of a dry spell in finding any decent ones... we're always out there working... right now, we don't have anything right now."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a neutral tone, focusing on the impact of strategic changes, division performance, and acquisition pipeline without pressing skepticism or overt concern.
  • Management maintained a confident but grounded tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A. Hermanns acknowledged challenges but said, "after three years of a steady decline, we're pretty hopeful that, that's over with," and emphasized the strength of the company’s business model.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to signaling stabilization, while analysts maintained a consistent, fact-seeking approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a significant strategic shift with the restructuring of MRI network and the launch of a $20 million share repurchase program, both absent in Q3.
  • Revenue, royalties, net income, and adjusted EBITDA all declined sequentially, with a sharper drop in Q4 versus Q3, while expense management, especially around workers’ compensation, further improved.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from questions about day labor stabilization and competitive wins (Q3) to the financial implications of the MRI transaction and M&A pipeline in Q4.
  • Management’s confidence in a market bottom and stabilization was more pronounced, while continuing to signal prudent acquisition discipline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hermanns acknowledged ongoing macro challenges and a multi-year decline in industry demand.
  • The divestiture of MRI’s permanent placement business could reduce revenue, but management stated the impact on income should be minimal due to its breakeven performance.
  • A dry M&A pipeline was noted, with Hermanns emphasizing the company is not pursuing deals for the sake of activity: "we're just simply not going to chase a deal just for the sake of having it."

Final Takeaway

Management highlighted that HireQuest remains profitable and adaptable despite industry headwinds, with a new MRI network ownership structure to focus resources and a $20 million share repurchase program reflecting confidence in long-term strategy. The company reports a stabilizing labor market and strong liquidity, reinforced by a debt-free position and ongoing dividend payments, while continuing to monitor acquisition opportunities with disciplined capital deployment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Peabody Energy plunges after slashing Centurion mine Q1 sales outlook
data.description:
Peabody Energy (BTU) sank 9.6% in Monday's trading after saying it expects Q1 sales volume from its Centurion mine in Australia will come in below prior expectations, anticipating ~250K tons compared with prior forecasts of ~700K tons, based on greater-than-expected challenges in commissioning of the mines.

Despite the revised forecast, Peabody (BTU) said its FY 2026 metallurgical coal volume targets remain unchanged at 10.3M-11.3M tons.

Jefferies analysts maintained a Buy rating on Peabody (BTU) shares, citing high seaborne thermal coal prices as a potential offset to the impact of the shipment delay.

"High seaborne thermal coal prices will help offset the impact of this ramp-up delay, but this is still likely to be an overhang on BTU shares until it is fully resolved," Jefferies analyst Christopher LaFemina said in a note, adding that a successful ramp of Centurion is a "key component of the Peabody investment case."

"While today's news may be disappointing, we still believe Centurion will be a driver of substantial free cash flow and earnings growth for Peabody over the medium/longer term," LaFemina wrote.

Coal companies that produce thermal coal, which is used for power generation, have been gaining recently as investors reprice demand for coal as a heating source because prices for oil and natural gas are rising in response to the Middle East war; Newcastle coal futures settled Monday at $144.25/ton, its best close since November 2024 and up 23% in March, according to Dow Jones.

data.title: Sangamo targets summer 2026 for Fabry BLA submission while advancing neurology pipeline and partner talks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sangamo Therapeutics (SGMO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Alexander Macrae stated that "Sangamo continued to make significant pipeline progress in 2025 and into the first quarter of '26. Set against a backdrop of regulatory and market uncertainty and with limited cash resources." He highlighted positive top line results from the registrational STAAR study in Fabry disease, with eGFR slope at 52 weeks, and confirmed that the FDA reiterated this may serve as the primary basis for accelerated approval.
  • Macrae reported that the rolling submission of a BLA to the FDA for ST-920, Sangamo's first wholly owned BLA, is in progress. Sangamo has also transitioned to a clinical stage neurology company, activating six clinical sites in the Phase I/II STAND study for chronic neuropathic pain.
  • Macrae announced a third neurology capsid license agreement, this time with Eli Lilly, to deliver genomic medicines for up to five CNS disease targets. The company has raised over $130 million since the start of 2025 through nondilutive license fees, milestone payments, and equity financing.
  • Nathalie Dubois-Stringfellow, Chief Development Officer, stated, "We have now submitted both the nonclinical and the clinical modules to the FDA. In addition, the antibody assay companion diagnostic...has been submitted to and accepted by the FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health." She added that the next milestone is completion of the chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) module, with manufacturing and testing of process validation lots completed and the first commercial lot manufactured.

Outlook

  • Macrae indicated that Sangamo anticipates "completing submission of the BLA as early as this summer, subject to our ability to secure adequate additional funding."
  • The company continues to seek a commercial partner for Fabry, which remains the top priority, with ongoing discussions involving multiple potential partners. Macrae described the process as complex and requiring extensive due diligence, stating, "Securing a commercial partner for Fabry remains our #1 focus, and we continue to engage in Fabry business development discussions with multiple potential partners."

Financial Results

  • Sangamo reported raising over $130 million in funding since the start of 2025 through nondilutive license fees, milestone payments, and equity financing, as stated by Macrae.
  • The company did not disclose specific quarterly revenue, EPS, or cash flow figures in the transcript.

Q&A

  • James Stamos, Jefferies LLC: "Can you provide more color on the revised timing for the Fabry BLA submission? What PPQ and other CMC-related activities are the primary gating factors here? ...can you comment on the status of the Fabry partnership discussions...?" Macrae responded that CMC is the critical path for filing and that timeline extensions are due to both agency requirements and prudent spending. Regarding partnerships, "most of the ones we spoke to last year have now gone. ...We are now speaking to multiple partners...these 3 are new to the discussions, and so we need to go through the process of due diligence, management presentations and then negotiations."
  • Luis Santos, H.C. Wainwright: "Have you had any additional interactions with the FDA recently or have any additional FDA interactions planned, specifically relating to the acceptance of the eGFR slope as a primary endpoint?" Macrae answered that the FDA reiterated in October the use of eGFR at one year for accelerated approval, and Sangamo does not plan further agency meetings unless there are regulatory changes.
  • Unknown Analyst: Asked about the timeline for the first in-human trial for STAC-BBB capsid and suitability of the capsid for different indications. Macrae explained that the choice of AAV9 for neuropathic pain was due to program initiation prior to STAC-BBB development and confirmed ongoing collaborations and positive relationships with partners utilizing STAC-BBB.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarity around regulatory timelines, partnership progress, and BLA submission, showing a slightly cautious to neutral tone with a keen interest in factors that may impact commercialization.
  • Management maintained a confident and factual tone, emphasizing progress and regulatory clarity while openly acknowledging challenges related to cash runway and partnership timing. Macrae stated, "we are following...very clear detailed pages of guidance on what was needed to complete the submission and maximize its chance of success," and "we will not be satisfied until we solve our long-term cash runway."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remains consistent, focusing on regulatory and partnership milestones, while management continues to demonstrate steady confidence but remains realistic about financial constraints.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter features advancement in the BLA rolling submission, with both clinical and nonclinical modules submitted, compared to the previous quarter's preparation phase.
  • The number of active clinical sites for the STAND study increased from two to six.
  • The company raised over $130 million in new funding, while the previous quarter focused on extending the cash runway with smaller milestones such as a $6 million license fee.
  • Strategic focus on Fabry commercialization partnership and neurology pipeline remains consistent, though management noted that new partners are now engaged versus those from last year.
  • Management and analysts maintain a similar tone, but there is increasing emphasis on the urgency of securing partnerships and funding.

Risks and Concerns

  • Macrae acknowledged that "we will not be satisfied until we solve our long-term cash runway," highlighting ongoing financial constraints.
  • The CMC module remains a gating factor for the BLA submission, requiring detailed compliance with FDA guidance.
  • Partnership negotiations are complex and time-consuming, with turnover among interested parties and continued regulatory uncertainty influencing the pace of deals.

Final Takeaway

Sangamo Therapeutics is progressing toward completing the BLA submission for its Fabry disease gene therapy as early as this summer, having already submitted key modules and advanced manufacturing requirements. The neurology pipeline is expanding, with six active clinical sites and a new Fast Track designation for a lead asset. Management continues to emphasize the need for additional funding and a commercial partner for Fabry, with new negotiations underway. The company highlights its regulatory engagement and pipeline achievements while being candid about the complexities and challenges ahead in securing long-term financial stability and partnership agreements.
